skillName: Position integers on a number line
misconceptionSummary: Students do not understand that equal intervals mean equal numerical steps.

visuals.ruleText: Equal physical gaps on a number line represent equal numerical steps
visuals.subText: If each gap is 10 wide, every gap adds exactly 10

steps.narration: A number line is a ruler for numbers. Equal physical gaps must represent equal numerical jumps — this is what makes the number line a fair measuring tool.

lines.text: Distance from zero = how big the number is

steps.narration: Every step right adds the same amount. Every step left subtracts the same amount. Position is not about how far from the edge of the page — it is about how far from zero.

lines.text: Find the nearest labelled marks on each side
Closer to 40 than 30? → past the midpoint

steps.narration: Use labelled marks as anchors. Decide which two your number falls between, then judge whether it is closer to the left anchor or the right one.

visuals.label: Equal gaps → anchor between two labels → judge closeness

steps.narration: Thirty-seven is seven of the ten steps from thirty to forty — past the midpoint, so it sits closer to forty. Anchoring to labels keeps the estimate accurate.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: placing numbers based on how they 'look' rather than counting equal steps. Every gap on a number line represents the same numerical jump.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Equal physical spacing means equal numerical spacing. Use labelled marks as anchors, then count equal steps from there.
